learn.title: Small Wind Turbines for Homes
learn.intro: Small wind turbines empower homes and communities with renewable energy, reducing reliance on fossil fuels. They are ideal for remote areas and can cut electricity bills by up to 50% when paired with batteries.
learn.c1_text: Small wind turbines use blades to capture wind energy, spinning a generator to produce electricity. A 10kW turbine can power a home using just 1,500 kWh/year at 12 mph average wind speed.
learn.c2_text: Turbines range from 400W micro units for cabins to 100kW systems for communities. Vertical-axis turbines suit urban areas, while horizontal-axis types thrive in rural settings with consistent wind.
learn.c3_text: India’s wind energy potential is 300 GW+ at 100m height. Small turbines can serve 30% of rural households lacking grid access, aligning with PM Surya Ghar Yojana goals.
learn.stat2: Global small wind market to reach $1.2B by 2030
learn.stat3: Turbines reduce CO2 by 1.5 tons/year per household

learn.title: Wind Turbine Electricity Generation
learn.intro: Understanding aerodynamics and generators reveals how wind turbines convert kinetic energy into clean power. This tech is critical for scaling renewable energy globally.
learn.c1_text: Blades shaped like airplane wings (airfoils) maximize lift. A 120m rotor blade sweeps 113,000 m², capturing 15% of wind energy at 12 m/s.
learn.c2_text: Gearboxes or direct-drive systems link blades to generators. A 2MW turbine spins at 15 RPM, generating 60Hz AC power via electromagnetic induction.
learn.c3_text: India’s wind capacity reached 44 GW (2024), with turbines operating at 25-30% capacity factors. The National Wind Energy Mission targets 300 GW by 2030.

learn.title: Hydrogen Colour Codes
learn.intro: Hydrogen’s colour codes (green, blue, grey) denote production methods. Green hydrogen, made via renewables, is key to decarbonizing industries.
learn.c1_text: Produced from natural gas with CO2 emissions. Accounts for 95% of global hydrogen, releasing 20 tons of CO2 per ton of hydrogen.
learn.c2_text: Generated via water electrolysis using solar/wind power. Costs ₹300-400/kg, with India aiming for 5 million tons/year by 2030.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Hydrogen Mission allocates ₹1 lakh crore for green hydrogen. Projects like Adani’s Gujarat plant target 100,000 tons/year by 2025.
learn.stat1: India: ₹1 lakh crore green hydrogen mission fund
learn.stat2: Global green hydrogen to hit 10M tons/year by 2030

learn.title: Floating Offshore Wind
learn.intro: Floating platforms unlock deep-water wind resources, boosting global offshore capacity. These structures operate in depths exceeding 150m.
learn.c1_text: Spar, semi-submersible, and tension-leg platforms stabilize turbines. A 15MW floating turbine generates 60GWh/year, enough for 20,000 homes.
learn.c2_text: Portugal’s WindFloat project uses semi-submersibles in 100m+ depths. Global floating capacity could reach 100GW by 2040.
learn.c3_text: India’s Exclusive Economic Zone has 1,000 GW offshore wind potential. Tamil Nadu’s pilot project aims for 1GW by 2030.

learn.title: Vehicle-to-Grid Tech
learn.intro: V2G technology lets EVs store and supply power to grids, enhancing stability. This innovation transforms EVs into mobile energy assets.
learn.c1_text: Bidirectional chargers enable EVs to discharge power. A 75kWh battery can power a home for 2 days, reducing peak grid demand by 15% during outages.
learn.c2_text: V2G provides frequency regulation and load balancing. In California, 10,000 EVs could shave 100MW off peak demand, cutting blackouts.
learn.c3_text: Indian EVs like Tata Nexon support V2G. Policies like FAME 2.0 incentivize 10% of EVs to have V2G by 2025.
learn.stat2: Global V2G market to reach $15B by 2030

learn.title: Solar Panel Types Compared
learn.intro: Monocrystalline, polycrystalline, and thin-film solar panels differ in efficiency, cost, and use cases. Choosing the right type impacts energy savings and ROI.
learn.c1_text: Made from single silicon crystals, these panels reach 22-24% efficiency. A 400W panel generates 1,800 kWh/year in India’s 5.5 kWh/m²/month sunlight.
learn.c2_text: Multi-silicon fragments lower costs but reduce efficiency to 15-17%. A 350W panel costs 15% less than mono, ideal for budget-conscious installations.
learn.c3_text: Used in India’s solar highways and rooftops, thin-film (10-13% efficiency) suits curved surfaces. Costs ₹30-40/W, with projects like Rajasthan’s Bhadla Park using hybrid systems.
learn.stat2: Global solar market to hit $300B by 2030
